summary: Qadar is the Islamic concept of divine decree or predestination, God's determination of what comes to pass. The relationship between God's decree and human free will and responsibility became one of the defining controversies of classical Islamic theology.
description:
Qadar refers to God's measuring out or determination of events. It is frequently paired with qaḍāʾ in the expression al-qaḍāʾ wa-l-qadar, together conveying the sense of the divine decree in both its eternal and its temporally enacted aspects. Belief in qadar is counted among the articles of faith in the standard Sunni formulation.

The central problem qadar raised was how to reconcile God's comprehensive power and knowledge with human moral responsibility. The early Qadariyya affirmed human free will, holding that human beings originate their own acts and are therefore accountable, a view developed and systematized by the Muʿtazila, who argued that divine justice requires that humans, not God, be the effective producers of their acts. Against this, others emphasized God's creation of all things, including human acts. The Ashʿarī school articulated an intermediate doctrine of kasb (acquisition): God creates the act and the power by which it is performed, while the human being acquires it, a formulation intended to preserve both divine omnipotence and human accountability. The precise causal content of kasb, and whether the Ashʿarī position amounts to occasionalism, the view that God is the only true cause, has been discussed by both classical theologians and modern scholars. The Māturīdī school offered its own account granting the human a real, if created, capacity for action. Each school's solution was criticized by the others as either compromising God's power or reducing human agency.
editorial_notes: This entry is scoped to qadar as the theological problem of divine decree and human action; it does not survey every school's technical vocabulary in detail. The positions of the Qadariyya, Muʿtazila, Ashʿariyya, and Māturīdiyya are described but not adjudicated, per the neutrality policy. The characterization of kasb and the occasionalism question follows the Stanford Encyclopedia of Philosophy entry on causation in Arabic and Islamic thought, which notes that scholars debate whether al-Ashʿarī's position constitutes full occasionalism, that dispute is reported here, not resolved. The label 'Qadariyya' was applied polemically and its historical referents are contested; this is flagged as a scoping simplification. To triangulate the Western academic sources with the tradition's own voice (neutrality principle #8), classical primary sources are appended: al-Ghazālī's al-Iqtiṣād for the Ashʿarī kasb doctrine and al-Shahrastānī's al-Milal for the heresiographical map of the schools; both are rated medium confidence as their relevant passages were not read line-by-line here.

relations.note: Debate over qadar is bound up with divine omnipotence and unity of God's acts, a dimension of tawhid.
Belief in qadar is included among the objects of faith in the standard Sunni formulation of iman.
The Muʿtazila held that human beings are the effective agents of their own acts, so that God's justice is preserved.
The Ashʿarī doctrine of kasb (acquisition) sought a middle path between free will and divine determination.
The name qadariyya was attached to those affirming human free will against a strongly predestinarian reading of qadar.

historical_period.note: The controversy emerged in the late 7th century with the Qadariyya and was systematized by the theological schools through roughly the 10th century CE.

sources.citation: Gardet, L. "al-Ḳaḍāʾ wa 'l-Ḳadar." In Encyclopaedia of Islam, 2nd ed., vol. 4, pp. 365–367. Leiden: Brill.

sources.citation: Winter, T. (ed.), The Cambridge Companion to Classical Islamic Theology. Cambridge University Press, 2008.

al-Ghazālī, Abū Ḥāmid. al-Iqtiṣād fī al-Iʿtiqād [Moderation in Belief]. Ashʿarī kalām work, early 12th c. CE. Expounds the doctrine that God is the sole creator of human acts and the accompanying created power by which they are 'acquired' (kasb), reconciling divine omnipotence with human accountability under qadar.

al-Shahrastānī, Muḥammad ibn ʿAbd al-Karīm. al-Milal wa-l-Niḥal [The Book of Sects and Creeds]. Classical heresiography (12th c. CE). Reports the positions of the Qadariyya, Muʿtazila, and their opponents on free will, human acts, and divine predetermination.
